coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric dual-emission carbon dots coupled with smartphone for visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA and biological sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Xiaorui
Shi, Lihong
Zhou, Wei
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescence analysis provides the feasibility for visual quantitative sensing by presenting continuous color changes. Herein, dual-emission carbon dots (D-CDs) have been developed for ratiometric recognition of Co2+ and EDTA, along with successive fluorescence variation of yellow, purplish-pink, purple, light blue, and dark blue. D-CDs have been synthesized by microwave-assisted heating of tetracycline and manifest dual emissions at 562 nm and 610 nm under 450 nm excitation. Intriguingly, the emissions at 562 nm and 610 nm are quenched while a ratio rising emission emerges at 482 nm upon introducing Co2+, which displays ratiometric fluorescence emission characteristics (I482/I562) in the range of 0.25–400 µM with significant fluorescence varying from yellow, purplish-pink, purple, light blue, to dark blue. Subsequent addition of EDTA could ratiometric recover the fluorescence of D-CDs in the range of 50–130 µM with the fluorescence returning from dark blue, light blue, purple, purplish-pink, to yellow. More crucially, integrating continuous color changes and color recognizer APP in the smartphone, visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA can be realized based on the ratio of blue value and red value (B/R) with linear ranges of 0–400 µM and 0–300 µM, respectively. Furthermore, proposed D-CDs could be extended as a visual biosensing platform for tracking Co2+ and EDTA in living cells, which have potentialapplication prospect in biosystem.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visual quantification
Co2+
EDTA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric detection and visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA
Characterization and FL properties of D-CDs
Ratiometric detection and visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA
Determination of Co2+ and EDTA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the synthesis of D-CDs and the applications for ratiometric detection, visual quantification and biological sensing of Co2+ and EDTA.

(A) TEM image of D-CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of D-CDs. (C) XPS full spectrum of D-CDs. (D − F) C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of D-CDs. (G) XRD pattern of D-CDs. (H) Raman spectrum of D-CDs. (I) UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectra of D-CDs.

(A) FL spectra of D-CDs with various amounts of Co2+ (0 – 400 µM). (B) Linear relationship between ln(I482/I562 + 0.13213) and Co2+ concentrations. (C) Linear relationship between B/R values and Co2+ concentrations from 0 μM to 400 μM. Inset: FL color changes of D-CDs solution in the presence of Co2+ (0 μM − 400 μM) under 450 nm excitation. (D) FL spectra of D-CDs@Co2+ with various concentrations of EDTA (0 – 300 µM). (E) Relationship between FL intensity ratios (I482/I562) and EDTA concentrations. Inset: linearity of response (50 μM − 130 μM). (F) Linear relationship between B/R values and concentrations of EDTA in the range of 0 μM − 300 μM. Inset: FL color changes of D-CDs@Co2+ solution along with EDTA (0 μM − 300 μM) under 450 nm excitation.
(A) Schematic diagram of the combination of D-CDs with Co2+. (B) Possible structure of the chelate formed by EDTA and Co2+.

Confocal fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with D-CDs (A), D-CDs@Co2+ (B) and D-CDs@Co2++EDTA (C) under 405 nm laser excitation. From left to right: channel 1 (λem = 425–500 nm), channel 2 (λem = 500–585 nm), channel 3 (λem = 585–685 nm), bright field, overlay of channel 1, channel 2, and channel 3, and ratio. (D) Intracellular FL intensities of HeLa cells incubated with D-CDs, D-CDs@Co2+ and D-CDs@Co2++EDTA. (E) Intracellular FL intensity ratios (Ichannel 1/Ichannel 2) of HeLa cells incubated with D-CDs, D-CDs@Co2+ and D-CDs@Co2++EDTA, respectively.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric dual-emission carbon dots coupled with smartphone for visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA and biological sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescence analysis provides the feasibility for visual quantitative sensing by presenting continuous color changes. Herein, dual-emission carbon dots (D-CDs) have been developed for ratiometric recognition of Co2+ and EDTA, along with successive fluorescence variation of yellow, purplish-pink, purple, light blue, and dark blue. D-CDs have been synthesized by microwave-assisted heating of tetracycline and manifest dual emissions at 562 nm and 610 nm under 450 nm excitation. Intriguingly, the emissions at 562 nm and 610 nm are quenched while a ratio rising emission emerges at 482 nm upon introducing Co2+, which displays ratiometric fluorescence emission characteristics (I482/I562) in the range of 0.25–400 µM with significant fluorescence varying from yellow, purplish-pink, purple, light blue, to dark blue. Subsequent addition of EDTA could ratiometric recover the fluorescence of D-CDs in the range of 50–130 µM with the fluorescence returning from dark blue, light blue, purple, purplish-pink, to yellow. More crucially, integrating continuous color changes and color recognizer APP in the smartphone, visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA can be realized based on the ratio of blue value and red value (B/R) with linear ranges of 0–400 µM and 0–300 µM, respectively. Furthermore, proposed D-CDs could be extended as a visual biosensing platform for tracking Co2+ and EDTA in living cells, which have potentialapplication prospect in biosystem.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent (FL) analysis has been widely utilized in environment, biology, medicine and other fields due to their simple operation, rapid response and high detection accuracy. The response of most FL sensors is relied on a single emission peak, which is easily disturbed by external factors, such as background signal, microenvironments around and local concentrations of probe molecules, photobleaching and complex conditions of real samples [1,2]. Ratiometric FL sensors exhibit encouraged self-calibration capability by calculating intensity ratio of two well-resolved emission peaks [3], effectively removing or reducing the interference and greatly improving the detection accuracy [4]. Additionally, ratiometric FL sensing can produce different FL colors with typical tonalities and thus lay the foundation for visual quantitative sensing. One key to realizing the revolution of ratiometric FL technique lies in the development of new FL sensing materials [5].

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging FL sensing material, have attracted increasing concern since they were accidentally discovered in 2004 [6]. Due to many prominent properties including facile preparation, tunable FL emission, eminent water solubility, superior stability, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity [7–9], CDs have been widely used in FL sensing [10–12], photoelectric devices [13], biosensing [14,15], smartphone based visual detection [16–18], anti-counterfeiting [19]. Moreover, CDs are usually combined with other materials to improve their performances and enrich their applications, such as nano-clay@CDs [20], CDs-gold nanoclusters [21]. However, the majority of CDs exhibit single emission characteristic and are usually built as single-emission intensity-changed sensors [22,23]. Some ratiometric FL sensors were designed by combining single-emission CDs with other phosphors. Gui et al. [24] reported a ratiometric FL sensor to detect ciprofloxacin by combining red-emission CDs and blue-emission SiDs. Bu et al. [25] developed a dual-emission ratiometric FL sensor to detect arginine and acetaminophen by combining blue-emission CDs with red-emission copper nanoclusters. Currently, a few of dual-emission CDs (D-CDs) for ratiometric FL sensing have been explored. Song et al. [26] exploited D-CDs for ratiometric discrimination of lysine and pH. Shi et al. [27] obtained D-CDs for ratiometric Al3+ sensing and cellular imaging. Li et al. [28] fabricated D-CDs and proved the application in ratiometric GSH sensing. However, the exploit of D-CDs-based ratiometric FL sensors is still in the initial stage and there is plenty of space for investigation.

Cobalt (Co) is an essential microelement for human body that plays a key role as a major component of vitamin B12 [29]. A tiny dose of Co2+ can boost the formation of red blood cells and prevent anaemia [30–32]. But, the excess of Co2+ may lead to hypotension, diarrhea, asthma, dermatitis, myocarditis, thyroid damage, gastrointestinal dysfunction and cancer [33–35]. Thus, it is highly momentous to find a handy and effective approach for Co2+ detection. Recently, CDs-based Co2+ FL sensors have been reported. Tian et al. [36] fabricated green-emission N-CDs with p-phenylenediamine and asparagine for Co2+ sensing. Liao et al. [37] designed blue-emissive P,N-CDs employing pyridoxal 5-phosphate and ethanediamine as carbon sources for the recognition of Co2+. Bano et al. [38] developed blue-emssion N-CDs by hydrothermal treatment of glycine and polyethyleneimine for Co2+ detection. However, these CDs-based Co2+ FL sensors are based on intensity-modulated feature. CDs-based Co2+ ratiometric FL sensors have hardly explored. Thus, it is imperative to develop CDs-based ratiometric FL sensors for Co2+ sensing.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), well-known chelator and clarificant, is widely used in detergents, eye-drops and anticoagulants [39]. Using EDTA can treat acute heavy metal poisoning [40]. However, it has been proved that EDTA may cause allergic dermatitis in humans, as well as hypoglycemia and hypocalcemia in the blood [40,41]. Hence, it is of extreme significance to detect the presence of trace amounts of EDTA. CDs-based FL sensors for EDTA detection have been reported. Yang et al. [39] constructed a nanosensor for EDTA and Zn2+ sensing by using Zn2+-passivated single-emissive CDs. Wen et al. [40] designed single-emission P,N-CDs as an “on–off-on” FL sensor for Co2+ and EDTA detecting. However, CDs-based EDTA ratiometric FL sensors have scarcely been reported. Therefore, it is necessary to develop CDs-based EDTA ratiometric FL sensor.

In this work, D-CDs have been fabricated through microwave-assisted heating of tetracycline. As-prepared D-CDs exhibit dual emissions at 562 nm and 610 nm. Intriguingly, the D-CDs displays intrinsic ratiometric FL variation (I482/I562) upon the introduction of Co2+ under one excitation wavelength of 450 nm, which is attributed to the coordination of Co2+ with D-CDs’ surface functional groups. And then, FL of D-CDs@Co2+ could be ratiometric recovered by EDTA due to the competitive capture of EDTA for Co2+ from D-CDs. Meanwhile, it endows D-CDs with visual quantitative detection capability to combine continuous FL color changes of yellow, purplish-pink, purple, light blue, and dark blue and a color recognizer application in the smartphone. Furthermore, owing to admirable stability, low cell toxicity and fine biocompatibility, proposed D-CDs have been successfully performed to observe fluctuations of intracellular Co2+ and EDTA (Fig. 1 ). Compared with those previously reported FL sensors, innovation points of this work lie in following aspects: (1) Ratiometric FL D-CDs were ingeniously developed by microwave-assisted method. (2) The D-CDs achieved visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA by integrating with smartphone. (3) Visual sensing of Co2+ and EDTA was accomplished in living cells.
Materials and instruments for experiment were mentioned in the supplementary material.

D-CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted heating of tetracycline. Briefly, 0.2 g tetracycline was added into 100 mL DDI water. Then the mixture was dispersed evenly by ultrasound and heated in a microwave oven (model: M1-L213B, raping time: 3 min, operational frequency: 2450 MHz) at 700 W for 15 min. Obtained suspension was centrifuged (3000 rpm, 20 min), filtered (0.22 μm microporous membrane), and dialyzed (500–1000 Da dialysis membrane, 12 h) to obtain D-CDs stock solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection and visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of Co2+ and EDTA was carried out in pH 7.0 phosphate buffered saline (PBS). Concretely, 80 μL of D-CDs stock solution (1.2 g L−1) was added to 300 μL of PBS, then, Co2+ standard solution with different concentrations were introduced. FL spectra and FL color changes of solution were recorded under 450 nm excitation. Then, FL colors were recognized through a color recognizer APP in the smartphone and transformed into RGB values. According to the linear relationship between the ratios of B value and R value (B/R) and Co2+ concentrations, the visual quantification of Co2+ can be realized.

For sensing of EDTA, different concentrations of EDTA standard solution were introduced to D-CDs@Co2+ (400 μM) solution. FL spectra and FL color changes of solution were recorded upon 450 nm excitation. The visual quantification of EDTA could be achieved through the linear relationship between B/R values and EDTA concentrations.

D-CDs have been synthesized through microwave-assisted heating of tetracycline. Compared with conventional preparation, the microwave-assisted strategy possesses excellent merits, such as low energy consumption, simple operation, rapid reaction, easy carbonization of raw materials, and environmental friendliness. Meanwhile, tetracycline is a broad-spectrum antibiotic with low toxicity. In addition, the tetracycline molecule has a large conjugated structure and contains carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen elements, which satisfied the demand of the synthesis of CDs and is suitable for preparing CDs. Water-soluble D-CDs were formed through decomposition, polymerization, aromatization, carbonization, and nuclear explosion under microwave radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and FL properties of D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and AFM images can clearly and intuitively display the size and morphology of D-CDs. Fig. 2 A shows that D-CDs possess quasi-spherical appearance and superior dispersity with the average particle size of 2.6 ± 0.4 nm through statistical analysis of 100 particles. Meanwhile, AFM images (Fig. S1) also illustrate that D-CDs have excellent dispersity with a mean height of 1.7 nm, which is coincident with TEM.

Surface structure of D-CDs were deeply investigated through FTIR and XPS. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2B) presents that there exist OH/NH (3028 cm−1), CH (2773 cm−1), COO− (1768 cm−1), NH/CN (1567 cm−1), COC (1216 cm−1), CO (915 cm−1) and CH (662 cm−1) on the surface of D-CDs. Meanwhile, the full XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C) shows that prepared D-CDs contains three elements: C, N and O, of which O belongs to adsorbed O [42]. C1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) contains three noticeable peaks: 284.9 eV for CC, 286.5 eV for CO and 288.2 eV for OCO. N1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) exhibits two peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.9 eV, corresponding to the presence of CN and NH. O1s spectrum (Fig. 2F) is separated into two distinct peaks, representing CO (531.9 eV) and COC/COH (533.0 eV). These results imply that there are OH, COOH, and NH2 on the surface of D-CDs. Moreover, XRD pattern of D-CDs (Fig. 2G) shows a typical broad peak at approximately 22° associated with the graphitic structure. In Raman spectrum of D-CDs (Fig. 2H), the peaks at 1388 cm−1 and 1571 cm−1 are attributed to the D (sp3-hybridized) and G (sp2-hybridized) bands, respectively. The intensity ratio of ID/IG is 0.95, indicating that the D-CDs contain a structure similar to graphite.

Fig. 2I shows UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectra of D-CDs. The absorption at 214 nm and 275 nm may derive from π − π* transition of CC while the absorption at 355 nm may relate to n − π* transition of CO. FL spectra of D-CDs demonstrate intriguing dual-emission behavior at 562 nm and 610 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, FL lifetime of D-CDs is 1.6622 ns and QY of D-CDs is 16.5 % using Rhodamine 6G as a reference.

FL stability of D-CDs was studied at different experimental conditions to assess their practicability. Fig. S2A proves that FL intensity of D-CDs maintains steady in alkaline condition with pH values from 7 to 10. As shown in Fig. S2B, FL intensity remains constant under different NaCl concentrations, indicating that FL intensity of D-CDs is not affected by ionic strength. Besides, FL intensity of D-CDs is almost invariant even after continuous irradiation for 100 min (Fig. S2C), representing strong photobleaching resistance. These phenomena illustrate that D-CDs have high stability, which endows them great potential in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection and visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the promising applications of prepared D-CDs in FL sensing, the responses of D-CDs to various ions and amino acids at pH = 7.0 were studied. FL intensity ratio (I482/I562) of D-CDs increases significantly in the existence of Co2+, but this phenomenon don’t occur when other analytes were added (Fig. S3A), which means that D-CDs have high selectivity for Co2+. In addition, other analytes don’t significantly interfere with Co2+ detection even at high concentrations (up to 400 μM) (Fig. S3A), which undoubtedly certify that as-acquired D-CDs could be developed as a ratiometric FL sensor to detect Co2+ with high anti-interference. Sensing ability of D-CDs for Co2+ was discussed within the scope of 0 – 400 µM under 450 nm excitation at pH 7.0. As seen in Fig. 3 A, with increasing Co2+ concentrations, FL intensities of D-CDs decrease progressively at both 562 nm and 610 nm while a new emission peak at 482 nm emerges and enhances gradually. Fig. 3B exhibits a fine linear relationship between ln(I482/I562 + 0.13213) and Co2+ concentrations in the scope of 0.25 μM − 400 μM (R2 = 0.996). The limit of detection (LOD) is 24.9 nM, which is significantly lower than that of reported CDs-based Co2+ sensors (Table S1).

Subsequently, various ions and amino acids at a concentration of 300 μM were introduced into D-CDs solution in the presence of 400 μM Co2+ at pH = 7.0 to investigate the selectivity of D-CDs@Co2+ system. Only EDTA makes FL intensity ratio (I482/I562) decrease considerably (Fig. S3B), implying that D-CDs@Co2+ has superior selectivity for EDTA. Additionally, other analytes don’t obviously interfere with EDTA detection even at high concentrations (up to 300 μM) (Fig. S3B), revealing high anti-interference of D-CDs@Co2+ system for EDTA. When EDTA concentration changes from 0 μM to 300 μM, FL intensity of D-CDs@Co2+ at 562 nm and 610 nm increases gradually, while that at 482 nm decreases progressively (Fig. 3D). A relationship is shown between FL intensity ratios (I482/I562) and EDTA concentrations in the range of 0 μM − 300 μM and a good linearity relationship is fitted in the range of 50 μM − 130 μM with R2 = 0.995 (Fig. 3E). LOD is 9.3 nM, which is significantly lower than that of previously reported CDs-based EDTA sensors (Table S2).

Interestingly, owing to the obvious color changes of the solution in the reaction, the smartphone imaging have been established for visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA. Fig. S4 illustrates the process of detecting Co2+ and EDTA based on the smartphone imaging. The color of D-CDs solution changes incrementally from yellow, purplish-pink, purple, light blue, to dark blue under 450 nm excitation with increasing Co2+ concentration from 0 μM to 400 μM (Inset of Fig. 3C). FL colors could be transformed into RGB values through a color recognizer application in the smartphone, of which the ratio of B value and R value (B/R) could be used as a parameter for Co2+ quantification. Fig. 3C shows a satisfactory linear relationship between B/R values and Co2+ concentrations from 0 μM to 400 μM, as well as LOD of 22.5 nM. Moreover, the color of D-CDs@Co2+ solution returns gradually from dark blue, light blue, purple, purplish-pink, to yellow under 450 nm excitation when EDTA concentration changes from 0 μM to 300 μM (Inset of Fig. 3F). And B/R values exhibit a good linear relationship with EDTA concentrations varying from 0 μM to 300 μM (Fig. 3F), as well as LOD of 17.2 nM. All above results reveal that D-CDs can be developed as optical mode and smartphone imaging platforms for ratiometric detection and visual quantification of Co2+ and EDTA.

Co2+ contains unfilled orbitals, which can accept lone pair electrons. The surface of D-CDs is covered by –OH, −COOH and −NH2, in which O and N have lone pair electrons. Therefore, upon adding Co2+ to D-CDs solution, Co2+ coordinates with O and N on the surface functional groups of D-CDs to form new complex. To confirm the conjecture, zeta potential and UV–vis absorption of D-CDs in the existence of Co2+ were performed. After introducing Co2+, zeta potential of D-CDs (Fig. S5) changes from −6.96 mV to +16.0 mV, probably attributing to the consumption of anions on the surface of D-CDs, and confirming the successful combination of Co2+ and D-CDs. Furthermore, UV–vis absorption (Fig. S6A) of D-CDs@Co2+ manifests that a new absorption peak at 400 nm emerges and its absorption intensity enhancesalong with increasing Co2+ concentrations, and the average FL lifetimes of D-CDs and D-CDs@Co2+ are calculated to be 1.6622 ns and 1.4570 ns based on the FL decay curves (Fig. S7), which further proves that D-CDs combine with Co2+. Simultaneously, upon introducing Co2+, average diameter of D-CDs increases from 2.6 nm to 5.2 nm (Fig. S8A), and average height increases from 1.7 nm to 8.9 nm (Fig. S9A), indicating that Co2+ induces the aggregation of D-CDs (Fig. 4 A). For one thing, combining Co2+ with D-CDs causes energy transfer [43], and thus the quenchment of yellow and orange FL. For another, Co2+-induced agglomeration of D-CDs restricts free rotation of surface functional groups and enhances π conjugation, inhibiting nonradiative transition, stimulating the radiative transition [44,45], and thus causing the appearance of a new blue emission.

EDTA has strong chelation ability. On adding EDTA to D-CDs@Co2+ solution, EDTA can capture Co2+ from D-CDs and chelate with Co2+ to form a more stable Co2+@EDTA chelate (Fig. 4B). To verify the proposed interaction, zeta potential and UV–vis absorption of D-CDs@Co2+ were determined in the presence of EDTA. Upon adding EDTA, zeta potential of D-CDs@Co2+ (Fig. S5) changes from +16.0 mV to −1.86 mV, the intensity of absorption peak at 400 nm gradually weakens until disappears with increasing EDTA concentrations (Fig. S6B), and the average FL lifetime of D-CDs@Co2+ returns to 1.6713 ns in the presence of EDTA (Fig. S7), indicating that Co2+ is separated from D-CDs. Meanwhile, on the addition of EDTA, average diameter of D-CDs decreases from 5.2 nm to 2.5 nm (Fig. S8B) and average height decreases from 8.9 nm to 3.0 nm (Fig. S9B), illustrating that the aggregated D-CDs release. Therefore, subsequently introducing EDTA to D-CDs@Co2+ system not only prevents energy transfer on the surface of D-CDs, causing the recovery of yellow and orange FL, but also weakens π conjugation, suppressing the radiative transition rate, enhancing nonradiative transition rate, and resulting in blue FL reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Co2+ and EDTA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical applicability of D-CDs, standard recovery experiments were carried out in tap water and lake water. The recoveries of Co2+ and EDTA in tap water are found to be 96.20–105.16 % and 92.68–101.88 %, respectively (Table S3). The recoveries of Co2+ and EDTA in lake water are 95.66–105.00 % and 94.89–104.95 % (Table S4), respectively. The results show that proposed D-CDs based sensor could be generalized to the detection of Co2+ and EDTA in real samples.

To employ the synthesized D-CDs in biological sensing, we used HeLa cells to perform MTT assay to appraise their cytotoxicity. Fig. S10 shows that cell survivalrate reaches more than 95 % even at 400 μg mL−1 of D-CDs, indicating that D-CDs have low cytotoxicity and are suitable for cellular imaging.

Ratiometric FL imaging of HeLa cells was achieved under 405 nm laser excitation by collecting three emission channels: blue (λem = 425–500 nm), yellow (λem = 500–585 nm) and orange (λem = 585–685 nm) channels. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, D-CDs-stained HeLa cells emit bright yellow and orange FL but weak blue FL. With the addition of Co2+, yellow and orange FL weaken while blue FL increases (Fig. 5B). On subsequent introducing EDTA, blue FL fades but yellow and orange FL enhance (Fig. 5C). Besides, Fig. 5D depicts the intracellular FL intensities of HeLa cells incubated with D-CDs, D-CDs@Co2+ and D-CDs@Co2++EDTA. And Fig. 5E illustrates intracellular FL intensity ratios (Ichannel 1/Ichannel 2) of HeLa cells incubated with D-CDs, D-CDs@Co2+, D-CDs@Co2++EDTA, respectively. These results confirm that D-CDs could be designed as a ratiometric FL sensing platform for recognizing Co2+ and EDTA in biological system.

Ratiometric FL D-CDs have been synthesized through microwave-assisted heating of tetracycline. Obtained D-CDs have been designed as a ratiometric FL sensing platform for sequential discrimination of Co2+ and EDTA in aqueous solution based on the change of FL intensity ratio (I482/I562) accompanied by reversible FL variation of yellow, purplish-pink, purple, light blue, and dark blue. Intriguingly, taking advantage of RGB values of FL recognized by a color recognizer APP in smartphone, quantitative detection of Co2+ and EDTA could be achieved. More interestingly, confocal FL microscopic images of HeLa cells demonstrate that as-prepared D-CDs can ratiometric visualize Co2+ and EDTA in living cells. It is anticipated that proposed protocol will show a bright prospect in visual quantification and biological sensing.

Xiaorui Dong: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Wei Zhou: Validation, Investigation. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108471.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthetic ratiometric fluorescent probes for detection of ions
Chem. Soc. Rev.

Gong, Ratiometric fluorescent sensors for sequential on-off-on determination of riboflavin, Ag+ and l-cysteine based on NPCl-doped carbon quantum dots, Anal. Chim. Acta 1144 (2021) 1–13.

Analyst

Adv. Mater.
